description: Estate/probate profile for claims, beneficiaries, witnesses, amendments, forfeiture conditions, identity ambiguity, and document evidence.

use_when: inheritance, estate, will, charter, probate, beneficiary, or forfeiture language
ambiguous initials or family identities in legal records
avoid_when: general family biography without legal consequence
medical fact intake or clinical advice
fictional scene narration without policy/rule language

semantic_ir_context: profile_scope: probate and estate-memory ingestion; separate claims, observed documents, rules, and legal conclusions
claim_policy: statements by beneficiaries are claims unless corroborated by an admitted document or witness rule; do not turn a claim into a world-state fact
witness_policy: amendment validity may depend on witness count, witness independence, and simultaneity; unresolved witness qualifications should quarantine or clarify
identity_policy: initials and surnames such as A. Silverton or R. Vale require identity grounding when multiple candidates exist
rule_policy: unless/except/only-if language should remain rule-like or unsafe unless the mapper can admit an executable rule clause
temporal_policy: preserve intervals, consecutive-duration conditions, and corrected dates; do not decide forfeiture from ambiguous location or identity

guardianship_policy: temporary guardianship, ordinary residence, and name-alias discoveries are probate state/evidence; do not terminate guardianship from an alias discovery unless the utterance explicitly says the condition is met
gift_condition_policy: conditional gifts may record direct declinations and explicit pass-to events, but rule-like alternatives should remain scoped to the source document

canonical_palette.notes: Scoped speech act; not truth of target.
Start date of an explicitly stated interval.
End date of an explicitly stated interval.
Ordinary residence status when explicitly stated.
Explicit refusal or declination of a role.
Explicit transfer/pass-to outcome in probate or gift source language.
Legal conclusion; should usually be query/rule-derived rather than directly asserted.
